<2026 Notes (History):
Applied in 2025, awaiting response in September.

We applied, but did not receive grant in 2024. Notes from Grants Manager James Bewley regarding our 2024 Application: "The most significant issue with that proposal was the lack of information about the program itself and the artists involved. We view everything through the quality of the artists’ experience with an organization – so are they pushing their practice forward, making new work, making work in new ways or in collaboration with new partners, etc. It can take many forms. But aside from the structural overview and mission of the org, we need much more detail about the artists you’re bringing in, why besides an interest in climate are they interested in working with you – and what will they be doing. Specifics are really important here – from the criteria of selection to what the artists are being supported to produce or present. The first couple of paragraphs of the proposal can set the stage, with that organizational info – but the bulk of the proposal should be focused on the artists, their projects, and any other programs or activities you have planned (that remain connected to the visual arts) from July 1 forward for two years (with as much detail as you have – for at least nine months out). If you can pull that together, it will make for a more competitive proposal." 

deadline@warholfoundation.org 

Grants range from $60,000-$100,000. The amount requested should be 25% of a project’s total direct costs or less.

What they Fund: The New York State Energy Research and Development Authority (NYSERDA) is seeking applications to fund and provide professional development training/mentoring for year-long, full-time fellowships, for individuals from Priority Populations and/or Disadvantaged Communities, to work within organizations and businesses that advance climate justice and clean energy priorities for disadvantaged communities. It is anticipated that fellows will work with community-based organizations, universities, municipalities, CTE organizations, tribal nations, public libraries, climate tech innovators/start-ups and clean energy businesses.
<2026 Notes (History):
Capped at $40,000; $37,000 toward an anual salary for the felow, $3,000 stipend for professional development/wrap around services for the Fellow. 

Eligible host employers must:

Be a community-based organization, university, municipality, clean energy business, or similar entity focused on climate justice in disadvantaged communities.
Offer a year-long, full-time (35-40 hrs/week) fellowship with professional development, training, and mentoring.
Have a physical business address in NYS and the legal authority to hire in NYS.
Employ at least two people (excluding owners or partners).
Provide comprehensive health benefits (medical, dental, vision) for the fellow.
Note: Some rules don't apply to certain certified or participating start-ups.

2026 Notes:
LOI Includes: 
-Organizational Description: Nature and purpose of HII.
-Project Statement: A concise statement of the need, objectives, and methods for the project.
-Personnel: Qualifications of the key staff (e.g., Tara DePorte and lead artists).
-Budget: A clear program budget.
-Other Support: A list of other funders already committed or being sought.
-IRS Rulings: Your 501(c)(3) and 509(a) status letters.
-Financials: Your most recent annual audit or financial statement.
-Board List: A list of HII’s officers and directors.

What they Fund:
The Kresge Foundation prioritizes initiatives that contribute to a more equitable and prosperous future in cities. Their focus areas include:
1. Arts & Culture: Programs that enhance access to arts and culture for diverse communities.
2. Education: Initiatives that promote educational equity and success for all.
3. Environment: Projects that address climate change, promote sustainability, and advance racial and economic justice.
4. Health: Programs that improve access to quality healthcare and promote healthy communities.
5. Human Services: Initiatives that support basic needs, strengthen families, and create pathways to opportunity.
6. Community Development: Projects that revitalize neighborhoods, create affordable housing, and foster economic development.
